verses.1: Imagine beginning any act — a journey, a meal, a prayer — by first surrendering it to the source of all mercy. This is not just an opening line; it is a doorway. Before a single word of praise is spoken, the reciter steps aside and invokes a name greater than their own — Allah, whose mercy is boundless and whose mercy is intimate, renewed moment by moment. Every word that follows in this surah rests on that foundation.
verses.2: Picture standing at the edge of everything that exists — galaxies, oceans, the unseen realms beyond human sight — and realizing all of it, without exception, belongs to and is sustained by One Lord. This praise is not a passive thank-you; it is the recognition that praise itself belongs to Him, woven into the fabric of creation. He is not merely Creator, but Sustainer, nurturing every atom of every world, seen and unseen, toward its purpose.
verses.3: Here the mercy named in the opening line returns, this time as description rather than invocation — as if to say: do not be afraid of the Lord of the worlds, for His dominion is wrapped entirely in compassion. Even His majesty does not overshadow His tenderness. Mercy is not a mood He enters; it is who He is.
verses.4: Now the tone shifts. The same Merciful Lord is also Master of a Day when every soul will stand utterly alone with its deeds. Justice and mercy meet in that Judgment; nothing is forgotten, nothing lost. It is a sobering reminder, spoken gently, that this life is not the whole story — there is an account still to come.
verses.5: This is the pivot of the entire surah — the moment the servant speaks directly to their Lord. You alone we worship, You alone we ask for help. No intermediary, no partner, no other refuge. It is a declaration of total dependence and total loyalty, spoken not as one voice but as we — joining every believer, past and present, in a single breath.
verses.6: And now comes the plea, urgent and humble: guide us to the straight path. Not a path already walked and forgotten, but one still being asked for, verse after verse, in every prayer — because guidance is not something owned once and kept forever. It is asked for and renewed, again and again, every single day.
verses.7: The final verse paints two roads and asks to be spared from both: the road of those who knew the truth yet turned away from it, drawing anger upon themselves, and the road of those who wandered without a compass, lost in confusion. What is asked for instead is the road already walked in blessing — the road of the prophets, the truthful, and the righteous, whose footsteps still light the way for anyone who recites these words today.

verses.1: Three lone letters open this surah — Alif, Lam, Meem — sounds without translation, a mystery left deliberately unexplained. It is as if the very first words of the Book announce that not everything here will yield to ordinary understanding; some things are simply meant to be received, not decoded.
verses.2: This is declared to be the Book — not a book, but the Book, with no doubt clinging to it anywhere within its pages. And its purpose is immediately named: guidance, but only for those whose hearts are already tuned toward carefulness and reverence, who are watching for how to live rightly.
verses.3: Picture a people who have never seen what they believe in, yet order their entire lives around it — who stand for prayer at fixed hours and give away a portion of what they've earned, simply because it was asked of them. Faith in the unseen, prayer, and generosity: these become the first visible signs of a heart that has said yes.
verses.4: These same people also believe in what has been sent to this Prophet and in every message sent before him, refusing to draw a line that separates one revelation from another. And they hold, without hesitation, that a life beyond this one is certain — not a comforting myth, but a fact they build their choices around.
verses.5: These are the ones, the verse says, who walk upon guidance sent directly from their Lord — and these are the ones who succeed. Success here is quietly redefined: not wealth, not status, but alignment with a guidance that comes from beyond oneself.
verses.6: Now the surah turns to a very different kind of heart. For those who have already decided to disbelieve, it makes no difference whether they are warned or left silent — the warning simply will not reach the place in them that would need to hear it.
verses.7: Their hearts, their hearing, their sight — Allah has sealed each one, as if a door that was left open too long against the truth eventually closes on its own. A tremendous punishment waits for them, not as some external whim, but as the natural end of a road they chose to walk.
verses.8: A third kind of person is introduced now — one who says, with perfect ease, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day,' yet inside carries no such belief at all. Words can be arranged into faith without a single beat of the heart ever agreeing to it.
verses.9: They imagine they are deceiving Allah and the believers around them, playing a clever game with divine truth. But the verse quietly corrects them: they deceive no one but themselves, and they cannot even feel it happening.
verses.10: There is a disease lodged in their hearts, and Allah lets it grow rather than shrink, because they chose to nurture it instead of confronting it. And what waits for them is a painful punishment, earned precisely because of the lies they kept telling.
verses.11: When they are told plainly, 'Do not spread corruption on the earth,' they respond with a straight face: 'We are only setting things right.' It is the oldest trick of self-deception — dressing harm up in the language of repair.
verses.12: But the truth stands apart from their self-image: they are the corrupters, though they cannot perceive it in themselves. Blindness to one's own harm is itself described here as a kind of punishment.
verses.13: When they are urged, 'Believe as the others have believed,' their pride answers back: 'Should we believe like fools believe?' Yet the verse turns the insult back on them — it is they, unknowingly, who are the foolish ones, dismissing wisdom because of who holds it.
verses.14: Among the believers, these people speak as believers. Alone with those who share their disbelief, they confess, 'We were only mocking them.' Two faces, worn depending on the room — this is the very definition of hypocrisy the surah is illustrating.
verses.15: Allah, in turn, mocks their mockery and lets them wander further, drunk on their own arrogance, blind to where the road actually leads. What they intended as manipulation becomes, in this telling, the mechanism of their own undoing.
verses.16: They have traded guidance for straying, and the verse pictures it as literally that — a purchase, a transaction, one path exchanged for another. But it was a losing trade: no profit came from it, and they never found the way home.
verses.17: Their situation is likened to someone who lights a fire in the darkness, and just as the light illuminates everything around them, it is suddenly taken away — leaving them in a blackness even deeper than before, unable to see anything at all.
verses.18: Deaf, dumb, and blind — not physically, but in the parts of themselves that matter most for finding truth. And because of this self-imposed shutdown, the verse says plainly, they will not return to the right path.
verses.19: A second image follows: a rainstorm from the sky, full of darkness, thunder, and lightning, so overwhelming that they stuff their fingers in their ears at every thunderclap, terrified of death itself. It is fear without faith — sensing danger everywhere but having no anchor to hold onto within it.
verses.20: The lightning flashes and, for a moment, lets them see just enough to take a few steps; then it plunges them back into darkness, and they freeze, unable to move at all. Allah could take their hearing and sight entirely, the verse warns, for He has power over absolutely everything — this precarious flickering is itself a form of mercy they do not recognize.
verses.21: The address widens now to all of humanity: worship your Lord, the One who created you and those who came before you, so that you might attain consciousness of Him. Worship is framed here not as a burden imposed from outside, but as the very reason for existing at all.
verses.22: This is the same Lord who made the earth a resting place and the sky a canopy overhead, who sends rain down and by it brings forth fruit for sustenance. And having laid out this evidence, the verse delivers its quiet challenge: do not set up rivals to Allah while knowing, deep down, that He alone did all of this.
verses.23: If anyone doubts that this Book truly comes from Allah, the challenge is direct: produce just one surah like it, and call on any helper you can find besides Allah to assist you. It is an invitation to test the claim, not merely accept it.
verses.24: And if that cannot be done — and the verse states plainly that it never will be — then a fire awaits, one whose fuel is people themselves alongside stones, prepared specifically for those who reject the truth. The warning is severe precisely because the stakes described are not small.
verses.25: But then the tone turns tender: give good news to those who believed and did what was right — gardens flow beneath them, and every time they are given fruit to eat, they remark on how familiar it looks to what they had before. Yet it is new every time, a paradise where even repetition is a delight rather than a monotony.
verses.26: Allah is not embarrassed, the verse says, to use even a mosquito as an example — or anything smaller — to illustrate truth. Believers recognize such comparisons as truth from their Lord, while disbelievers ask what possible purpose such a tiny image could serve, and in that very question, some are led astray while others are guided.
verses.27: Those who are led astray by it are the ones who break their covenant with Allah after it was firmly made, who sever bonds Allah has commanded be joined, and who spread corruption throughout the earth. Loss, the verse concludes, belongs entirely to them.
verses.28: How, the verse asks with real astonishment, can anyone disbelieve in Allah, when you were once lifeless and He gave you life? He will cause you to die, then bring you back to life again, and to Him you will all be returned in the end — the entire arc of existence sketched in a single breath.
verses.29: It was He who created everything on the earth for humankind, and then turned His attention to the heaven, forming it into seven heavens in perfect order. And He has full knowledge of all things — nothing in this vast design is outside His awareness.
verses.30: Allah announces to the angels a plan they cannot yet fully understand: 'I am placing a successive authority on the earth.' The angels, puzzled, ask why He would place there one who will spread corruption and shed blood, when they themselves glorify and sanctify Him constantly — and Allah answers simply, 'I know what you do not know.'
verses.31: Adam is taught the names of all things, a knowledge given directly, not earned through trial. He is then presented to the angels and told, 'Inform Me of the names of these, if you are truthful' — a test designed to reveal the gap between their worship and his potential.
verses.32: The angels answer with humility rather than pretense: 'Exalted are You; we have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed, it is You who is the Knowing, the Wise.' Their honesty about their own limits becomes, in itself, a form of praise.
verses.33: Adam is told to inform them of the names, and when he does, Allah turns back to the angels: 'Did I not tell you that I know the unseen of the heavens and the earth, and I know what you reveal and what you conceal?' The test was never really about names — it was about revealing a knowledge in Adam that even the angels did not possess.
verses.34: The angels are commanded to prostrate before Adam, and every one of them does — except Iblis, who refuses out of arrogance and becomes, in that single refusal, one who disbelieves. A command obeyed instantly by beings of pure light is rejected by one who let pride override it.
verses.35: Adam and his wife are told to dwell in the garden and eat freely from wherever they wish, with only one tree marked as forbidden, lest they become wrongdoers. Paradise here is described as abundance with a single, deliberate boundary.
verses.36: Satan causes the two of them to slip from the garden, and they are sent down from the state they had been in, told they will have enmity between them, and a temporary home and provision on earth. What had been effortless closeness to Allah now becomes a life that must be worked toward.
verses.37: Adam receives words from his Lord — words of repentance — and Allah accepts his turning back, for He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful. The fall is not the end of the story; it becomes the very moment mercy is demonstrated.
verses.38: All are told to descend from the garden together, and a promise is made: guidance will come, and whoever follows it will have no fear and no grief. The exile is softened by an open door back, offered to everyone who comes after.
verses.39: Those who disbelieve and deny the signs are named as companions of the Fire, to remain there. The same verse that offers a way home also names, plainly, what happens to those who refuse to walk through it.
verses.40: The Children of Israel are addressed directly now: remember the favor bestowed upon you, and fulfill your covenant with Me, so that I will fulfill Mine. A relationship built on mutual faithfulness, not one-sided obligation.
verses.41: They are urged to believe in what has now been sent down, confirming what they already carry, and warned not to be the first to reject it, nor to trade its truth for a small price. Recognizing something they already knew, in a new form, was the test.
verses.42: 'Do not mix truth with falsehood or conceal the truth while you know it' — a warning against the subtle sin of blending honesty with distortion until the line between them disappears. Knowing the truth and hiding it is treated here as its own distinct wrong.
verses.43: They are commanded to establish prayer, give charity, and bow with those who bow — to join the community of worship rather than stand apart from it. Faith is framed not as a private conviction alone but as participation.
verses.44: A sharp question follows: do you order righteousness upon others while forgetting it yourselves, even as you recite the Scripture? It exposes the particular emptiness of teaching a truth one does not live.
verses.45: Seek help, the verse instructs, through patience and prayer — though this is indeed difficult except for the humbly submissive. It names outright that this path is not easy, and that ease comes only through humility.
verses.46: Those who are humbly submissive are described as ones who are certain they will meet their Lord and that to Him they will return. That certainty is what makes the difficulty of patience and prayer bearable.
verses.47: The Children of Israel are told again to remember the favor bestowed on them, and that Allah preferred them over the worlds of their time — a reminder meant to stir gratitude, not superiority.
verses.48: They are warned of a day when no soul will avail another soul at all, when no intercession will be accepted, no compensation taken, and no help given. Every relationship of mutual aid that exists in this life is described as dissolving entirely on that day.
verses.49: Allah reminds them of when He saved them from Pharaoh's people, who were afflicting them with the worst torment — slaughtering their sons and letting their women live — a great trial from their Lord. Deliverance is recalled here as something that followed real, sustained suffering.
verses.50: He recalls parting the sea for them, saving them, and drowning Pharaoh's people while they watched. It is presented as a favor witnessed with their own eyes, not something merely told to them afterward.
verses.51: Moses was given forty nights, and in his absence they took up worship of the calf, wronging themselves in the process. The very people who had just watched a sea part turned, within weeks, to an idol.
verses.52: And yet, the verse says, Allah pardoned them after that, so that they might be grateful. Forgiveness follows almost immediately after the failure is named, without minimizing what had happened.
verses.53: Moses was given the Scripture and the criterion for distinguishing right from wrong, so that they might be guided. A book and a standard for judgment, given together, as tools for a people still finding their footing.
verses.54: Moses said to his people, 'You have wronged yourselves by taking the calf, so repent to your Maker and kill yourselves' — a severe act of repentance that their Maker then turned toward them and accepted, for He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful. Even the harshest command in this passage ends in the same word: mercy.
verses.55: They once said, 'We will not believe you until we see Allah outright,' and a thunderbolt seized them while they were looking on. Their demand for undeniable proof was met, but not in the way that left them comfortable.
verses.56: Then they were raised up after their death, so that they might be grateful. Even this near-total consequence circles back to the same invitation — gratitude, not punishment, as the intended response.
verses.57: Allah shaded them with clouds and sent down manna and quails, telling them to eat from the good things provided. They did not wrong Him by their later ingratitude, the verse notes; they wronged only themselves.
verses.58: They were told to enter a town and eat freely from it as they wished, to enter its gate bowing humbly, and to say a word of repentance — and in return their sins would be forgiven, with more given to those who did good. A simple act of humility was offered as the price of forgiveness.
verses.59: But the wrongdoers among them substituted another word for the one they were told to say, and so a plague was sent down on them from the sky because of their defiance. A small act of substitution — one word swapped for another — is treated here as a real betrayal.
verses.60: When Moses asked for water for his people, he was told to strike the stone with his staff, and twelve springs gushed out, each group knowing its own drinking place. Provision here is exact and personal, measured out to each of the twelve tribes by name.
verses.61: They grew tired of one kind of food and asked Moses to request a variety — lentils, onions, garlic — for which Moses asked why they would exchange what was better for what was worse. Humiliation and poverty struck them for their persistent disbelief and killing of prophets without right, the verse says, because they had continually transgressed and disobeyed.
verses.62: Believers, Jews, Christians, and Sabians alike — whoever truly believes in Allah, the Last Day, and does righteous work will have their reward with their Lord, with no fear and no grief. The measure given here is sincerity and action, not the label a community carries.
verses.63: Their covenant was taken, and Mount Sinai was raised above them like a canopy, with the instruction to hold firmly to what had been given and remember its contents, so that they might become righteous. The image of a mountain suspended overhead makes vivid just how seriously that covenant was meant to be taken.
verses.64: Yet they turned away after that, and were it not for Allah's favor and mercy upon them, they would have been among the losers. The rescue from ruin is named as grace, not something they had secured on their own.
verses.65: Those among them who transgressed the Sabbath were told, 'Be apes, despised.' It stands as one of the starkest images in the surah — a community reduced to something almost unrecognizable because of a boundary they refused to respect.
verses.66: That punishment was made an example to those who witnessed it and those who came after, and a lesson for the righteous. Even a severe consequence is framed here as instruction meant to reach far beyond the moment itself.
verses.67: Moses told his people that Allah commanded them to slaughter a cow, and they responded, 'Do you take us in mockery?' — to which Moses answered, 'I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant.' A simple command becomes, through their reluctance, an unexpectedly long negotiation.
verses.68: They asked Moses to clarify what kind of cow was meant, and he explained it should be neither old nor young, but in between — so they were told to do as they were commanded. Each new question delays the simple act a little further.
verses.69: They asked further what color it should be, and were told it should be a bright yellow, pleasing to those who look at it. The specificity of the answer only seems to invite yet another question.
verses.70: Still unsatisfied, they said the cows all resemble one another to them, and asked for guidance once more, adding, 'we will be guided, if Allah wills.' Even sincere-sounding words can mask a reluctance to simply act.
verses.71: Finally they were told it should be a cow not trained to plow the earth or irrigate the field, sound and without blemish — and they said, 'now you have come with the truth,' and they slaughtered it, though they almost did not. The verse leaves a quiet irony hanging: the truth had been there from the very first instruction.
verses.72: A man had been killed, and they disputed about it, but Allah was to bring out what they were concealing. The cow's slaughter, it becomes clear, was never really about a cow at all — it was tied to uncovering the truth of a hidden crime.
verses.73: They were told to strike the slain man with part of the sacrificed cow, and in this way Allah brings the dead to life and shows His signs, so that people might use reason. What seemed an unrelated command turns out to be the very key to revealing what had been hidden.
verses.74: After all of this, their hearts hardened, becoming like stones or even harder — for among stones are some from which rivers gush, some that split open releasing water, and some that fall from fear of Allah, while their hearts did none of these things. Even inanimate rock, the verse suggests, can respond to Allah in ways their hearts refused to.
verses.75: The Prophet is asked whether he really hopes they will believe him, when a party of them used to hear the words of Allah and then distort them knowingly, after having understood them. Distortion here is not ignorance but a deliberate choice made by people who knew better.
verses.76: When they meet the believers, they say, 'We believe,' but alone with one another they ask, 'Do you inform them of what Allah has revealed to you, so they may argue with you about it before your Lord?' Their own community questions the wisdom of showing believers too much.
verses.77: But do they not know, the verse asks, that Allah knows what they conceal as well as what they declare? The very whispering meant to stay hidden is described as already fully known.
verses.78: Among them are unlettered ones who do not know the Scripture except in wishful thinking, and they rely only on assumption. A gap opens here between claiming a heritage and actually understanding what it teaches.
verses.79: Woe to those who write the Scripture with their own hands and then claim, 'This is from Allah,' in order to sell it for a small price — woe to them for what their hands have written, and woe to them for what they earn from it. The condemnation falls twice: once for the act, once for the profit taken from it.
verses.80: They say the Fire will not touch them except for a few numbered days, and they are asked whether they have taken a covenant with Allah — for He would never break His covenant — or whether they are simply saying about Allah what they do not know. Comfortable assumptions about mercy are challenged here for having no real foundation.
verses.81: Whoever earns sin and finds themselves surrounded by their wrongdoing, the verse says, will remain in the Fire forever. A single verse draws the boundary between those who are simply flawed and those who let their wrongdoing become the whole shape of their life.
verses.82: Those who believe and do righteous deeds are named companions of Paradise, remaining there forever — the mirror image offered right beside the warning. The two fates sit side by side, so neither can be mistaken for the only possible outcome.
verses.83: A covenant was taken from the Children of Israel: worship none but Allah, show excellence to parents, relatives, orphans, and the needy, speak good to people, establish prayer, and give charity — and then most of them turned away, except a few. The list reads almost like a complete description of a decent life, and still it proved hard to keep.
verses.84: Another covenant followed: do not shed each other's blood or expel one another from your homes — and they affirmed it, bearing witness themselves. A promise made with full awareness, witnessed by their own testimony.
verses.85: Yet here they are, the verse says, killing one another and expelling a group of their own people, cooperating against them in sin, even while ransoming them back when captured — as if some parts of the covenant mattered and others did not. Selective obedience is treated here as no obedience at all; whoever does this earns only disgrace in this life and the severest punishment in the next.
verses.86: These are the ones who bought the life of this world at the price of the Hereafter — for them the punishment will not be lightened, nor will they be helped. A trade made in this life, the verse warns, cannot be undone once its terms are settled.
verses.87: Moses was given the Scripture, and messengers followed after him one after another; Jesus was given clear proofs and strengthened with the Holy Spirit — yet every time a messenger came with something that did not suit their desires, some were arrogantly rejected, some accused of lying, and some killed. A pattern repeats across generations: truth arriving, and desire deciding whether to receive it.
verses.88: They say, 'Our hearts are wrapped in coverings' — but it is their disbelief that has sealed them this way, and only a few of them truly believe. What sounds like an excuse is revealed as the very condition it claims to explain.
verses.89: When a Book came from Allah confirming what they already possessed — even though they had once prayed for victory through it over the disbelievers — they rejected it the moment they recognized it. The curse of Allah is described as resting on those who deny something they once longed for.
verses.90: How wretched is what they sold themselves for, the verse says: denying what Allah revealed, resentful that He would send His favor upon whom He wills among His servants. Envy, not disbelief in itself, is named as the hidden engine behind their rejection.
verses.91: When told to believe in what Allah has sent down, they answer, 'We believe in what was sent down to us,' while rejecting what came after, even though it confirms the very truth they hold. If they truly believed what they claim to, the verse asks, why did they kill the prophets of Allah before?
verses.92: Moses came to them with clear proofs, and still they took up the calf in his absence, wronging themselves. Clear evidence, the passage shows again, does not by itself guarantee faithfulness.
verses.93: Their covenant was taken and the mountain raised above them: 'hold firmly to what We have given you, and listen.' They answered, 'We hear and we disobey' — and love of the calf was made to seep into their hearts because of their disbelief, a phrase that captures obedience in word only.
verses.94: They claim the abode of the Hereafter is theirs alone, exclusively, to the exclusion of all other people — so they are told, if that is true, then wish for death, if they are being truthful. It is a challenge meant to expose whether the claim is sincerely held or merely spoken.
verses.95: But they will never wish for it, the verse says, because of what their own hands have sent forth — and Allah is fully aware of the wrongdoers. Their unwillingness to face death becomes, itself, quiet testimony against their claim.
verses.96: You will find them, of all people, most attached to life — even more than those who associate partners with Allah. Each of them wishes to be granted a thousand years, though even that would not save them from the punishment, for Allah sees everything they do.
verses.97: Say: whoever is an enemy to Gabriel — it was he who brought the Qur'an down upon your heart by Allah's permission, confirming what came before, as guidance and good news for the believers. Their objection to the message becomes, in effect, an objection to the very act of delivering it.
verses.98: Whoever is an enemy to Allah, His angels, His messengers, Gabriel and Michael — Allah, in turn, is an enemy to those who disbelieve. Hostility toward the message is treated here as hostility toward its Source.
verses.99: Clear signs have been sent down, and none disbelieve in them except the defiantly disobedient. The verse locates the failure not in the clarity of the signs but in the deliberate refusal to accept them.
verses.100: Is it not so, the verse asks, that every time they make a covenant, a party among them throws it aside? Indeed, most of them do not believe — a pattern of broken promises stretched across their whole history.
verses.101: When a messenger came to them from Allah, confirming what they already had, a group of those given the Scripture threw the Book of Allah behind their backs, as if they did not know. Familiarity with scripture, the verse shows, offers no guarantee of honoring it.
verses.102: They followed instead what the devils recited during the reign of Solomon — though Solomon himself did not disbelieve; it was the devils who disbelieved, teaching people magic, along with what was sent down to two angels in Babylon, Harut and Marut, who taught no one without first warning, 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve.' Even a warning attached to forbidden knowledge could not stop people from seeking it out anyway, and by it they learned only what could harm and not benefit them.
verses.103: Had they believed and been mindful of Allah instead, a reward from Him would have been far better, if only they had known. The verse leaves the better path visible right beside the one they chose.
verses.104: Believers are told not to say a certain ambiguous word to the Prophet that could be twisted into mockery, but to say something clearer instead, and to listen — for the disbelievers face a painful punishment. Even the choice of a single word, the verse shows, can carry real weight.
verses.105: Neither the disbelievers among the People of the Scripture nor the polytheists wish that any good be sent down to you from your Lord — yet Allah selects for His mercy whom He wills, for He is the possessor of great bounty. Their resentment cannot obstruct a generosity that was never theirs to control.
verses.106: Whatever verse Allah abrogates or causes to be forgotten, He brings one better than it or similar to it — did you not know that Allah has power over all things? Revelation itself is described here as something living, adapting to what a community needs at a given time.
verses.107: Do you not know that to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and that besides Him you have no protector and no helper? The question is less about information and more about where one has actually placed their trust.
verses.108: Or do you wish to question your Messenger as Moses was questioned before? Whoever exchanges faith for disbelief has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way — a reminder that endless demands for proof can themselves become a form of resistance.
verses.109: Many among the People of the Scripture wish they could turn you back to disbelief after your belief, out of envy from within themselves, even after the truth has become clear to them. Yet believers are told to pardon and overlook until Allah delivers His command, for Allah is over all things competent.
verses.110: Establish prayer and give charity, and whatever good you send forth for yourselves, you will find it with Allah — for He is Seeing of what you do. Nothing offered sincerely, the verse promises, is ever truly lost.
verses.111: They say none will enter Paradise except one who is Jewish or Christian — this is their wishful thinking. Say: produce your proof, if you should be truthful — a demand for evidence returned upon those who first offered only assumption.
verses.112: Rather, whoever submits himself entirely to Allah while being a doer of good will have his reward with his Lord; no fear will there be upon them, nor will they grieve. Belonging to a particular label is replaced here with sincerity of submission as the true measure.
verses.113: The Jews say the Christians stand on nothing, and the Christians say the Jews stand on nothing — while both recite the same Scripture. Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ, leaving the final verdict to the One who actually knows.
verses.114: Who does more wrong than one who prevents the mention of Allah's name in places of worship and strives toward their destruction? Such people, the verse says, have no right to enter them except in fear — for them is disgrace in this life and a great punishment in the next.
verses.115: To Allah belongs the east and the west, so wherever you turn, there is the face of Allah — He is all-Encompassing and Knowing. No direction, the verse suggests, is ever truly outside His presence.
verses.116: They say Allah has taken a son — exalted is He above that; rather, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and all are devoutly obedient to Him. The claim of a son is set against a much larger picture of complete, total ownership.
verses.117: He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth, and when He decrees a matter, He simply says to it, 'Be,' and it is. Creation itself is described here not as effort but as a single, unhindered word.
verses.118: Those without knowledge ask why Allah does not speak to them directly, or why a sign does not come to them — those before them said the same thing, their hearts similar to theirs. Yet clear signs have already been made plain for people who are certain, and certainty, the verse implies, is what was really missing.
verses.119: The Prophet was sent with the truth as a bringer of good news and a warner, and he will not be questioned about the companions of the blazing Fire. His task is delivery, not the outcome of every heart that hears him.
verses.120: Neither the Jews nor the Christians will ever be pleased until you follow their religion — say: the guidance of Allah is the only true guidance. And if you were to follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you would have no protector or helper against Allah.
verses.121: Those to whom the Scripture was given and who recite it as it should truly be recited — those are the ones who believe in it; whoever disbelieves in it, those are the losers. The measure again is not possession of a text but genuine engagement with it.
verses.122: The Children of Israel are told once more to remember the favor bestowed upon them, and that Allah preferred them over the worlds — a reminder repeated because gratitude, the surah suggests, needs to be renewed, not just recalled once.
verses.123: They are warned of a day when no soul will avail another soul at all, when no compensation will be accepted from it, no intercession will benefit it, and none will be helped. The same warning given earlier returns here, underscoring how total that day's isolation will be.
verses.124: Abraham was tried by his Lord with certain commands, which he fulfilled completely, and Allah said, 'I will make you a leader for the people.' Abraham asked, 'And of my descendants?' and was told, 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers' — leadership here is shown to be earned, not simply inherited.
verses.125: The House was made a place of return and security for the people, with the instruction to take the standing place of Abraham as a place of prayer. Abraham and Ishmael were commanded to purify the House for those who circle it, seclude themselves there, and bow and prostrate.
verses.126: Abraham prayed, 'My Lord, make this a secure city and provide its people with fruits, whoever believes among them in Allah and the Last Day.' Allah answered that He would grant enjoyment for a while even to those who disbelieved, before compelling them to the punishment of the Fire — a fate described as evil indeed.
verses.127: As Abraham and Ishmael raised the foundations of the House, they prayed, 'Our Lord, accept this from us; indeed You are the Hearing, the Knowing.' A monumental act of construction becomes, in their own words, a small offering hoping only to be accepted.
verses.128: They continued, 'Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and from our descendants a nation submissive to You; show us our rites of worship and accept our repentance, for You are the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.' Even while building the House itself, their prayer reached far beyond the stones in front of them, toward generations they would never see.
verses.129: They prayed further, 'Our Lord, send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses, teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them — indeed You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise.' A prayer offered by Abraham for a people yet unborn, answered generations later in a single man in Mecca.
verses.130: Who would turn away from the religion of Abraham except one who fools himself? Abraham was chosen in this world, and in the Hereafter he will be among the righteous — a legacy so significant that later communities each tried to claim it exclusively for themselves.
verses.131: When his Lord said to him, 'Submit,' he replied, 'I have submitted to the Lord of the worlds.' No hesitation, no negotiation — a single word met with a single, immediate answer.
verses.132: Abraham instructed his sons in this same religion, and so did Jacob: 'My sons, Allah has chosen this religion for you, so do not die except while in submission.' A father's final concern, expressed here, is not wealth or legacy but the state in which his children will meet death.
verses.133: Were you witnesses when death approached Jacob, when he said to his sons, 'What will you worship after me?' They said, 'We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham, Ishmael, and Isaac — one God, and to Him we submit.' A deathbed question becomes a declaration passed intact from one generation to the next.
verses.134: That was a nation that has passed on; it will have what it earned, and you will have what you earn, and you will not be asked about what they used to do. Each generation, the verse insists, is judged on its own record, not that of its ancestors.
verses.135: They say, 'Be Jews or Christians, and you will be guided' — say instead: rather, the religion of Abraham, inclining toward truth, for he was not among those who associated others with Allah. A claim to exclusive guidance is answered by reaching back before either community existed.
verses.136: Say: we believe in Allah and what was revealed to us, and what was revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the descendants, and what was given to Moses and Jesus, and what was given to all the prophets from their Lord — we make no distinction between any of them, and to Him we submit. A single sentence holds together an entire lineage of revelation without dividing it.
verses.137: If they believe in the same as you believe in, then they have been guided; but if they turn away, they are only in dissension, and Allah is sufficient for you against them, for He is the Hearing, the Knowing. The measure of true guidance offered here is shared belief, not shared label.
verses.138: Take on the color, the dye, of Allah — and who is better than Allah in coloring? It is Him we worship. Faith is imagined here as something that stains a life all the way through, rather than sitting on its surface.
verses.139: Say: do you argue with us about Allah, when He is our Lord and your Lord? We have our deeds and you have your deeds, and we are sincere to Him alone. A dispute that could easily spiral is quietly closed by drawing a simple, respectful boundary.
verses.140: Or do you say that Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, and the descendants were Jews or Christians? Say: do you know better, or does Allah? And who does more wrong than one who conceals a testimony they have received from Allah? Claiming a historical figure for one's own sect, the verse suggests, requires overlooking the very timeline that makes the claim impossible.
verses.141: That was a nation that has passed on; it will have what it earned, and you will have what you earn, and you will not be asked about what they used to do. The same closing refrain returns once more, sealing this long historical passage with the same reminder: every soul answers only for itself.
verses.142: The foolish among the people will ask what turned the believers away from the direction they used to face in prayer — say: to Allah belong the east and the west, and He guides whom He wills to a straight path. A change of direction becomes, for some, an opportunity to question rather than to understand.
verses.143: This community is made a just, median nation, so that it may be a witness over other people, and the Messenger a witness over this community. The change of the prayer direction was only a test, to distinguish who would follow the Messenger from who would turn back on their heels — a hard thing, except for those Allah has guided.
verses.144: The Prophet's face was seen turning toward the sky in hope, and so the direction he loves is given — turn your face toward the Sacred Mosque. Those given the Scripture know it is the truth from their Lord, and Allah is not unaware of what they do.
verses.145: Even if every sign were brought to those given the Scripture, they would not follow this direction, nor would the Prophet follow theirs, nor would they follow one another's. A change in orientation, the verse shows, was never really about geography at all.
verses.146: Those given the Scripture recognize this truth as they recognize their own sons, yet a party among them conceals it while knowing full well. Familiarity this intimate makes the concealment all the more deliberate.
verses.147: The truth is from the Prophet's Lord, so he is told, do not be among the doubters. A simple, firm anchor offered right in the middle of so much dispute.
verses.148: For every community there is a direction it faces, so race one another in good deeds — wherever you are, Allah will bring you all together, for He is capable of all things. The argument over direction dissolves into a far more urgent competition.
verses.149: From wherever you set out, turn your face toward the Sacred Mosque, for it is truly the truth from your Lord — Allah is not unaware of what you do. The instruction repeats, this time addressed personally to the Prophet as he travels.
verses.150: From wherever you set out, turn your face toward the Sacred Mosque, and wherever you all are, turn your faces toward it, so that people will have no argument against you — except those who are unjust among them, so fear them not, and fear Me, that I may complete My favor upon you and that you may be guided.
verses.151: Just as a messenger was sent among you from yourselves, reciting Our verses to you, purifying you, and teaching you the Book and wisdom, teaching you what you did not previously know. The whole passage on the direction of prayer resolves into this deeper gift — a teacher sent from among the people themselves.
verses.152: Remember Me, and I will remember you; be grateful to Me and do not deny Me. A relationship is offered here in the simplest possible terms — remembrance answered by remembrance.
verses.153: Believers are told to seek help through patience and prayer, for Allah is with the patient. A brief instruction, repeated from earlier, now placed right before a passage about real hardship.
verses.154: Do not say of those slain in the path of Allah that they are dead — rather, they are alive, though you do not perceive it. Death in that particular cause is described here as something other people simply cannot yet understand from where they stand.
verses.155: Believers will certainly be tested with something of fear and hunger, and a loss of wealth, lives, and fruits — but give good tidings to the patient. The trials named are not hypothetical; they are the ordinary hardships of a real life.
verses.156: Those who, when calamity strikes them, say, 'Indeed we belong to Allah, and indeed to Him we will return' — this single sentence becomes, in the verses that follow, the marker of who the patient truly are.
verses.157: Those are the ones on whom are blessings from their Lord and mercy, and those are the rightly guided. A response spoken in a moment of loss becomes the very thing that earns divine mercy in return.
verses.158: Safa and Marwa are among the symbols of Allah, so whoever makes pilgrimage to the House or performs umrah, there is no blame upon him for walking between them. What some had hesitated over as tradition is affirmed here as an act with sacred standing.
verses.159: Those who conceal the clear proofs and guidance We sent down, after We made them clear to people in the Scripture, are cursed by Allah and cursed by those who curse. Concealing something meant to be shared is treated with unusual severity here.
verses.160: Except for those who repent, correct themselves, and make clear what they concealed — those, Allah will accept their repentance, for He is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful. Even the harshest condemnation in this passage leaves a door open.
verses.161: Those who disbelieve and die while disbelieving — upon them is the curse of Allah, the angels, and all of humanity together. A single choice, unchanged until death, is described as drawing condemnation from every level of creation.
verses.162: They will abide therein eternally; the punishment will not be lightened for them, nor will they be reprieved. The permanence described here is total, without pause or exception.
verses.163: And your God is one God — there is no deity except Him, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful. After such a heavy passage, the surah returns, almost gently, to this simple center.
verses.164: In the creation of the heavens and the earth, the alternation of night and day, the ships that sail the sea for people's benefit, the rain that revives dead land and scatters creatures across it, and the shifting of winds and clouds between sky and earth — in all of this are signs for people who use reason. A single verse gathers an entire natural world as evidence, addressed to anyone willing to think it through.
verses.165: Yet some people take equals to Allah, loving them as they should love Allah — but those who believe love Allah more intensely. Even the strongest attachments people form are measured here against a love meant to exceed them.
verses.166: If only those who were followed could see, when they disown those who followed them, and the bonds between them are cut off completely. Relationships built on false devotion are shown here dissolving at the very moment they are needed most.
verses.167: Those who followed will say, 'If only we had another turn, that we might disown them as they have disowned us.' Thus will Allah show them their deeds as regrets upon them, and they will never emerge from the Fire. A wish for a second chance arrives too late to matter.
verses.168: People are told to eat from what is lawful and good on the earth, and not to follow the footsteps of Satan, for he is a clear enemy. A permission this broad — eat what is lawful and good — is paired with a warning about the one influence that would corrupt it.
verses.169: He only orders you to evil and immorality, and to say about Allah what you do not know. What seems like freedom in following such urges is revealed as a narrow, specific harm.
verses.170: When told to follow what Allah has revealed, some answer, 'We will follow only what we found our fathers doing' — even if their fathers understood nothing and were not guided. Inherited habit is challenged here for its own sake, apart from whether it was ever grounded in truth.
verses.171: The example of those who disbelieve is like one who calls out to something that hears nothing but a shout and a cry — deaf, dumb, and blind, they do not use reason. Rejecting truth is described here not as an act of intelligence but as a kind of self-imposed silence.
verses.172: Believers are told to eat from the good things provided for them and to be grateful to Allah, if it is truly Him they worship. Gratitude is offered as the natural companion to provision, not a separate obligation.
verses.173: What is forbidden is only carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and what has been dedicated to other than Allah — but whoever is forced by necessity, without desiring it or transgressing, no sin is upon him, for Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Even a strict prohibition carries within it an allowance for genuine need.
verses.174: Those who conceal what Allah has sent down in the Book and exchange it for a small price consume nothing into their bellies but fire — Allah will not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection, nor purify them, and for them is a painful punishment. Concealing guidance for personal gain is treated here as one of the gravest betrayals in the surah.
verses.175: Those are the ones who bought error in exchange for guidance and punishment in exchange for forgiveness — how patient they must be to face the Fire. The verse marvels, almost, at how deliberately such a costly trade could be made.
verses.176: That is because Allah sent down the Book in truth, and those who differ over the Book are in extreme dissension. Disagreement itself is not condemned here so much as the refusal to let truth settle it.
verses.177: Righteousness is not turning your faces toward east or west, but the righteous are those who believe in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets, and give their wealth, despite loving it, to relatives, orphans, the needy, travelers, those who ask, and for freeing captives, and who establish prayer, give charity, fulfill their promises, and are patient in poverty, hardship, and battle — those are the ones true in faith, and they are the righteous. One of the surah's most comprehensive verses redefines an entire debate about direction into a portrait of a fully lived faith.
verses.178: Retribution is prescribed for you in cases of murder — the free for the free, the slave for the slave, the female for the female — but whoever receives pardon from his brother, then let there be a suitable follow-up and payment with good conduct; this is a lightening from your Lord and a mercy. What could read as harsh law is shown, in the same breath, to make room for forgiveness.
verses.179: In retaliation there is life for you, people of understanding, so that you may become righteous. Justice, here, is framed as something that protects life rather than merely punishing its loss.
verses.180: When death approaches one of you, if he leaves wealth, a bequest is prescribed for parents and relatives according to what is acceptable — a duty upon the righteous. Even at the edge of life, the surah is concerned with how care continues afterward.
verses.181: Whoever alters a bequest after hearing it, the sin is only upon those who alter it — Allah is Hearing and Knowing. Responsibility here rests entirely on whoever tampers with someone else's final wishes, not on the one who made them.
verses.182: If one fears injustice or sin from the person making the bequest and corrects the matter between the parties, there is no sin upon him — Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Even the person who intervenes to fix an unfair bequest is shown mercy for stepping in.
verses.183: Fasting is prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you, that you may become righteous. A practice is described here as something ancient, shared across generations of believers long before this one.
verses.184: Fasting is for specified days; whoever among you is ill or on a journey may make up the same number on other days, and those who find it a hardship may feed a poor person instead — though whoever volunteers extra good, it is better for him, and fasting is best for you, if you only knew. Flexibility is built directly into the command, so that no one is left without a way to fulfill it.
verses.185: The month of Ramadan is that in which the Qur'an was revealed, a guidance for the people and clear proofs of guidance and criterion — so whoever witnesses the month should fast it, while the ill or traveling make up the days later; Allah intends ease for you and does not intend hardship, so that you complete the period and glorify Him for guiding you, and that you may be grateful. The command to fast is wrapped, from the very start, in a stated preference for ease over difficulty.
verses.186: When Allah's servants ask about Him, He is near — He responds to the call of the supplicant when he calls upon Him. So let them respond to Him and believe in Him, that they may be rightly guided — nearness this immediate is offered right in the middle of the passage on fasting.
verses.187: It is made permissible on the nights of the fast to approach your wives, for they are clothing for you and you are clothing for them — Allah knew you were deceiving yourselves, so He turned to you in mercy and pardoned you. Now you may associate with them and seek what Allah has decreed, and eat and drink until the white thread of dawn becomes distinct from the black thread, then complete the fast until night, without approaching your spouses while secluded in devotion at the mosques.
verses.188: Do not consume one another's wealth unjustly, nor offer it to authorities in order to consume a portion of others' wealth sinfully while you know. Corruption disguised through official channels is condemned here just as clearly as any other kind of theft.
verses.189: People ask about the crescent moons — say: they are markers of time for people and for pilgrimage. Righteousness is not entering houses from the back out of some superstitious ritual, but righteousness is fearing Allah; so enter houses through their doors, and fear Allah, that you may succeed.
verses.190: Fight in the cause of Allah those who fight you, but do not transgress — Allah does not love transgressors. Even the permission to fight is bound tightly by a limit on how far that fighting may go.
verses.191: Kill them wherever you overtake them and expel them from wherever they have expelled you, for persecution is worse than killing — but do not fight them near the Sacred Mosque unless they fight you there; if they do, then kill them, for that is the recompense of disbelievers. A difficult permission is granted here specifically as a response to prior persecution, not as an open invitation.
verses.192: But if they cease, then Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Even mid-conflict, the door back to mercy is kept open the instant hostility stops.
verses.193: Fight them until there is no more persecution and worship is devoted to Allah; but if they cease, let there be no aggression except against oppressors. The stated goal of the fighting is named plainly here, and it is not conquest for its own sake.
verses.194: The sacred month is for the sacred month, and sanctities are subject to reciprocity — so whoever transgresses against you, respond in kind, but fear Allah and know that Allah is with those who fear Him. Restraint, even here, is placed just above the permission to respond.
verses.195: Spend in the cause of Allah and do not throw yourselves into destruction with your own hands, and do good, for Allah loves the doers of good. A caution against recklessness is placed directly beside the call to sacrifice for a greater cause.
verses.196: Complete the hajj and umrah for Allah — and describes in detail the provisions for those who cannot, whose sacrifice or fasting substitutes, and the rulings for combining the two rites, ending with a warning to fear Allah, who is severe in punishment. Even the fine legal detail of pilgrimage is framed around consciousness of Allah rather than mere ritual completion.
verses.197: Hajj is during well-known months; whoever undertakes it should engage in no obscenity, no wickedness, and no disputing during hajj — and whatever good you do, Allah knows it. Take provisions, but the best provision is righteousness — so fear Allah, O people of understanding.
verses.198: There is no sin upon you in seeking bounty from your Lord during the pilgrimage season — and when you depart from Arafat, remember Allah at the sacred site, and remember Him as He has guided you, for you were before this among those astray. Ordinary commerce and sacred remembrance are shown here as able to coexist within the same journey.
verses.199: Depart from the place from which the people depart, and ask forgiveness of Allah, for Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. A simple instruction to move with the collective, rather than set oneself apart even in ritual.
verses.200: When you have completed your rites, remember Allah as you used to remember your forefathers, or with a much more intense remembrance — for among the people are those who say, 'Our Lord, give us in this world,' and they will have no share in the Hereafter. Even amid ritual devotion, the verse notes that some hearts are still only asking for this world alone.
verses.201: But others pray for good in this life and good in the next, and to be shielded from the Fire — a fuller, more balanced request than the one before it, refusing to abandon either world for the other.
verses.202: Those are the ones who will have a real share earned from what they asked for, and Allah is swift in settling accounts. The reckoning is not slow or uncertain; it is already in motion.
verses.203: Remember Allah during the appointed days, and whoever hurries away after two days commits no sin, nor does whoever stays longer, so long as it is done in awareness of Him. Even the small logistics of pilgrimage are folded back into the same purpose of remembrance.
verses.204: Among people is one whose talk about worldly life is dazzling, who even calls Allah to witness what is in his heart, yet he is the fiercest of opponents underneath. Eloquence, the verse warns, is no proof of sincerity.
verses.205: When he turns away, he moves through the land working to spread ruin, destroying crops and livestock — Allah does not love corruption. The contrast between his polished words and his actual effect on the earth could not be sharper.
verses.206: When he is told to be mindful of Allah, pride seizes him in his wrongdoing, so Hell is enough for him — a truly wretched resting place. Told to change course, he only digs in further.
verses.207: And among people is one who sells himself seeking Allah's pleasure, and Allah is Kind to His servants. Set right beside the corrupt man is his opposite: someone who gives everything, expecting nothing back but Allah's approval.
verses.208: Believers are called to enter into submission completely, not partially, and not to follow in Satan's footsteps, for he is a clear enemy. Faith is asked for as a whole commitment, not a set of convenient fragments.
verses.209: And if anyone slips back after clear proofs have already come, let them know that Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise — the warning is stated plainly, without threat or drama, as simple fact.
verses.210: Are they waiting for nothing less than Allah Himself to come to them in canopies of cloud, with the matter already decided? All things return to Allah in the end regardless of how long anyone waits to accept that.
verses.211: Ask the Children of Israel how many clear signs We gave them — and whoever exchanges Allah's favor after it has reached him will find Allah severe in punishment. A whole history of evidence is compressed into a single rhetorical question.
verses.212: The life of this world is made to appear beautiful to those who disbelieve, and they look down on those who believe, yet the believers will be above them on the Day of Resurrection — Allah provides without limit to whoever He wills. Present appearances and final outcomes are shown here to run in opposite directions.
verses.213: Mankind was once a single community; then Allah sent prophets bearing good news and warning, and sent the Book with them in truth to judge between people in their disagreements. Guidance did not arrive because people were already united — it arrived because they had already begun to differ.
verses.214: Do you suppose you will enter Paradise without first facing what those before you faced? They were shaken by hardship and adversity until the messenger and the believers with him cried out, asking when Allah's help would come — and indeed, Allah's help is always near. The question is put directly to whoever assumes faith should come easily.
verses.215: They ask what they should spend — say: whatever good you give should go to parents, relatives, orphans, the needy, and travelers; and whatever good you do, Allah is fully aware of it. Charity is redirected here from an abstract virtue into a very specific list of people.
verses.216: Fighting has been made obligatory for you, though it is hateful to you — but perhaps you dislike something that is good for you, and love something that is bad for you, for Allah knows and you do not. Human aversion and divine wisdom are placed side by side, and the verse trusts the latter over the former.
verses.217: They ask about fighting during the sacred month — say it is a great sin, but turning people away from Allah's path, disbelief in Him, and driving people from the Sacred Mosque are greater still in Allah's sight; and persecution is worse than killing. Whoever turns back from their faith and dies a disbeliever will have their deeds come to nothing in this world and the next.
verses.218: Those who believed, emigrated, and strove in Allah's cause may hope for His mercy — Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. The verse closes a heavy passage on conflict with a note of tenderness toward those who bore the weight of it.
verses.219: They ask about wine and gambling — say there is great harm in both, along with some benefit for people, but the harm far outweighs the benefit. They also ask what they should spend — say: whatever is beyond your needs. Two very different questions, about vice and about giving, are answered in the same breath, each with the same instinct to weigh cost against benefit.
verses.220: On matters of this world and the next — and they ask about orphans; say that improving their condition is best, and if you combine your affairs with theirs, they are your brothers; Allah knows the corrupt from the reformer, and had He willed, He could have made this difficult for you. What could have been a burdensome rule is instead framed as something Allah has deliberately made workable.
verses.221: Do not marry idolatresses until they believe — a believing slave woman is better than a free idolatress, even if she pleases you; and do not give believing women in marriage to idolaters until they believe — a believing slave is better than a free idolater, even if he pleases you. Such people invite toward the Fire, while Allah invites toward Paradise and forgiveness by His leave, making His verses clear so people may reflect.
verses.222: They ask about menstruation — say it is a vulnerable time, so keep apart from wives during it and do not approach them until they are purified; when they are purified, come to them as Allah has directed you, for Allah loves those who turn to Him often and those who keep themselves clean. A private, sensitive question receives a direct, practical, and unembarrassed answer.
verses.223: Wives are described as a place of cultivation for you, so come to your cultivation whenever you wish, and send good ahead for yourselves; be mindful of Allah, and know that you will meet Him — give good news to the believers. Intimacy is folded into the same vocabulary as sowing and future harvest, planting the idea that today's choices bear fruit later.
verses.224: Do not let your oaths by Allah become an excuse to avoid righteousness, mindfulness of Him, or making peace between people — Allah is Hearing and Knowing. Piety is not allowed to hide behind the letter of a vow.
verses.225: Allah does not hold you responsible for careless oaths, only for what your hearts have truly intended — Allah is Forgiving, Forbearing. A distinction is drawn here between a slip of the tongue and a real commitment of the heart.
verses.226: Those who vow to abstain from their wives have four months to decide; if they reconcile within that time, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. A cooling-off period is built directly into the law, protecting a marriage from a rash vow made in anger.
verses.227: But if they resolve on divorce, Allah is Hearing and Knowing — the same four months either restores the marriage or clarifies that it has genuinely ended, leaving no one in limbo.
verses.228: Divorced women must wait three menstrual cycles, and it is not lawful for them to hide what Allah has created in their wombs, if they truly believe in Allah and the Last Day; their husbands have the better right to take them back in that time if they seek reconciliation. Women have rights corresponding to their obligations, according to what is fair, though men have a degree above them — Allah is Exalted in Might, Wise.
verses.229: Divorce may be revoked twice; after that, either keep the wife in a fair manner or release her with kindness — it is not lawful to take back anything you have given her, except where both fear they cannot uphold Allah's limits, in which case there is no blame if she offers something in return for her release. These are Allah's limits, so do not transgress them, for whoever does has wronged themselves.
verses.230: If he divorces her a third time, she is not lawful for him again until she marries another man; if that man then divorces her, there is no blame on the first husband and wife returning to each other, if they believe they can uphold Allah's limits — these are His limits, made clear for people who understand. A deliberately high barrier is placed here against treating divorce and remarriage as something casual.
verses.231: When you divorce women and they approach the end of their waiting period, either retain them fairly or release them fairly — do not keep them out of spite to harm them, for whoever does has wronged himself. Do not take Allah's revelations lightly; remember His favor upon you and the guidance He has sent to instruct you, and be mindful of Him, knowing He is fully aware of everything.
verses.232: When you divorce women and they reach the end of their term, do not prevent them from remarrying their husbands if they mutually agree in a fair manner. This is instruction for whoever among you believes in Allah and the Last Day — it is more virtuous and pure for you, and Allah knows while you do not know.
verses.233: Mothers may nurse their children for two complete years, for those who wish to complete the nursing period; the father must provide for their food and clothing in a fair way, and no one should be burdened beyond their capacity. Neither parent should be harmed on account of the child, and the same duty extends to an heir; if the parents choose weaning by mutual consent, there is no blame on them.
verses.234: Those who die and leave wives behind — the wives must wait four months and ten days; once their term is completed, there is no blame on you for what they decide for themselves in a fair way, and Allah is fully aware of what you do. A defined mourning period gives space and clarity rather than leaving the widow's status uncertain.
verses.235: There is no blame if you hint at a proposal to widowed women, or keep it private, for Allah knows you will think of them — but do not make a secret promise, only speak in an honorable way, and do not resolve on the marriage contract until the prescribed period is over. Know that Allah knows what is in your hearts, so be wary of Him, and know that He is Forgiving, Forbearing.
verses.236: There is no blame on you if you divorce women you have not touched or fixed a dowry for, but still provide for them in a fair way — a duty upon those who do good. Even a marriage that ends before it truly began still carries an obligation of decency.
verses.237: If you divorce them before consummation but after setting a dowry, give them half of what was specified, unless they forgo it or the one holding the marriage contract chooses to forgo it — forgoing is nearer to righteousness. Do not forget generosity between yourselves, for Allah sees everything you do.
verses.238: Guard carefully over your prayers, and the middle prayer especially, and stand before Allah in devout obedience. A brief instruction on prayer interrupts the long passage on marriage and divorce, as if to recenter everything around worship.
verses.239: If you are in fear, pray while walking or riding, and when you are safe again, remember Allah as He has taught you what you did not previously know. Even the most disrupted circumstances are not allowed to excuse someone from remembrance entirely.
verses.240: Those who die and leave wives behind should bequeath a year's maintenance for them without expelling them from their home, but if they leave on their own there is no blame for what they do with themselves in a fair way — Allah is Exalted in Might, Wise.
verses.241: Divorced women are entitled to a fair provision, a duty upon those who are mindful of Allah — a small closing obligation that keeps the theme of care attached even to a relationship's end.
verses.242: This is how Allah makes His revelations clear to you, so that you may understand. A short verse pauses the flow of legislation just to name what all of it has been for.
verses.243: Have you not considered those who left their homes by the thousands, fearing death, only to be told by Allah to die — and then He gave them life again? Allah is full of bounty to people, yet most of them do not give thanks.
verses.244: Fight in the cause of Allah, and know that He is Hearing and Knowing — a brief command placed right after a story about people who tried, and failed, to outrun death by fleeing from duty.
verses.245: Who is it that will loan Allah a goodly loan, which He will multiply for them many times over? Allah withholds and grants abundantly, and to Him you will all be returned. Generosity toward others is described here in the language of a transaction with guaranteed, multiplied return.
verses.246: Have you not considered the leaders of the Children of Israel after Moses, when they asked a prophet of theirs to appoint a king for them so they could fight in Allah's cause? Yet when fighting was decreed for them, most of them turned away — Allah knows the wrongdoers well.
verses.247: Their prophet told them Allah had appointed Talut as king; they objected that he had no great wealth and greater right belonged to them — the prophet answered that Allah had chosen him and increased him in knowledge and stature, and Allah gives His kingdom to whom He wills. A test of leadership begins with a challenge to their own assumptions about who deserves it.
verses.248: Their prophet told them the sign of his kingship would be the return of the ark, carrying reassurance from their Lord and relics left by the family of Moses and Aaron, carried by the angels — in that is a sign for you, if you are truly believers. Proof arrives, but only for those already willing to see it as proof.
verses.249: When Talut set out with his forces, he warned that Allah would test them with a river — whoever drank from it was not with him, and whoever refrained, tasting only a little, was with him. Most drank freely; when Talut and the believers with him crossed, others said they had no strength that day against Goliath's army — but those certain of meeting Allah replied that many a small company has overcome a large one by Allah's permission, and Allah is with the steadfast.
verses.250: When they came out to face Goliath and his forces, they prayed for patience to be poured over them, for their feet to be made firm, and for help against a disbelieving people — the whole battle is entered through a prayer rather than a battle cry.
verses.251: By Allah's permission they defeated them, and David killed Goliath; Allah gave David kingship and wisdom, and taught him what He willed. Were it not for Allah restraining some people through others, the earth would be completely corrupted — but Allah is full of bounty to all the worlds.
verses.252: These are the verses of Allah, recited to you in truth, and you are certainly among the messengers — the long historical narrative closes by turning back to address the one now reciting it.
verses.253: Of these messengers, some were raised above others; some Allah spoke to directly, and some He raised in rank; Jesus, son of Mary, was given clear proofs and strengthened with the Pure Spirit. Had Allah willed, those after them would not have fought one another after clear proofs came to them, but they differed — some believed and some disbelieved, and had Allah willed, they would not have fought, yet Allah does what He intends.
verses.254: Believers are told to spend from what they have been provided before a Day comes with no more trading, no friendship to lean on, and no intercession to rely on — and it is the disbelievers who are the wrongdoers. Opportunity is framed here as something with a closing date.
verses.255: Allah — there is no god but Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of all existence; neither drowsiness nor sleep overtakes Him; everything in the heavens and the earth belongs to Him; no one can intercede with Him except by His permission; He knows what is before people and what is behind them, and they grasp nothing of His knowledge except what He wills; His throne extends over the heavens and the earth, and preserving them tires Him not, for He is the Most High, the Most Great. Widely regarded as the single most exalted verse in the whole book, it stands alone in the middle of ordinary legislation like a sudden window opened onto the sky.
verses.256: There is no compulsion in religion — the right path has been made distinct from error; whoever rejects false gods and believes in Allah has grasped the firmest handhold, one that will never break, and Allah is Hearing and Knowing. Belief, the verse insists, has to be reached rather than forced.
verses.257: Allah is the ally of those who believe, bringing them out of darkness into light; as for those who disbelieve, their allies are false gods, who bring them out of light into darkness — those are the companions of the Fire, remaining there forever. Two opposite trajectories, toward light and away from it, are laid out as a single choice.
verses.258: Have you not considered the one who argued with Abraham about his Lord, simply because Allah had given him a kingdom, when Abraham said his Lord gives life and causes death, and the man claimed he too could give life and cause death? When Abraham said Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west instead, the disbeliever was left stunned — Allah does not guide wrongdoing people.
verses.259: Or consider the one who passed by a town fallen into ruin, its roofs collapsed, and asked how Allah could bring it back to life after its death; Allah caused him to die for a hundred years, then raised him and asked how long he had stayed — he answered a day or part of a day; Allah told him he had stayed a hundred years, and showed him his food and drink unspoiled while his donkey's bones were shown to him and then clothed in flesh before his eyes, so that he might know Allah has power over all things.
verses.260: And when Abraham said, my Lord, show me how You give life to the dead, Allah asked if he did not already believe — Abraham said yes, but he wanted his heart put at ease; Allah told him to take four birds, tame them to himself, place a part of each on separate hills, then call them, and they would come to him swiftly, so that he would know Allah is Exalted in Might, Wise. Certainty of faith and the human wish to see it made tangible are shown here as fully compatible.
verses.261: The example of those who spend their wealth in Allah's cause is like a seed that grows seven ears, each ear containing a hundred grains — Allah multiplies for whom He wills, and He is All-Encompassing, Knowing. Generosity is pictured here not as loss but as planting.
verses.262: Those who spend their wealth in Allah's cause, then do not follow their spending with reminders of it or with harm, will have their reward with their Lord, and no fear will come upon them, nor will they grieve. The gift is only complete once it is given without strings attached.
verses.263: Kind words and forgiveness are better than a charity followed by injury — Allah is Free of need, Forbearing. Generosity that wounds the receiver's dignity is quietly ranked below simple kindness with nothing given at all.
verses.264: Believers are told not to nullify their charities with reminders of generosity and hurtful words, like one who spends his wealth only to be seen by others and does not believe in Allah or the Last Day — his likeness is a smooth rock covered in dust, struck by heavy rain and left bare; such people gain nothing from what they have earned, and Allah does not guide disbelieving people.
verses.265: The example of those who spend their wealth seeking Allah's pleasure and out of certainty in their own souls is like a garden on high ground — heavy rain strikes it and it doubles its yield, and if no heavy rain reaches it, a light shower suffices, for Allah sees everything they do. Sincerity, not the size of the gift, is what determines whether it flourishes.
verses.266: Would any of you wish to have a garden of palm trees and grapevines, with rivers flowing beneath it and every kind of fruit in it, and then old age comes upon him with weak children to support, and a whirlwind with fire strikes the garden and burns it entirely? This is how Allah makes His verses clear, so that you may reflect — the loss of a lifetime's effort at the worst possible moment becomes a warning against generosity spoiled by pride or show.
verses.267: Believers are told to spend from the good things they have earned and from what Allah has produced for them from the earth, and not to seek out the inferior things to give away, things they themselves would only accept with reluctance and narrowed eyes — and know that Allah is Free of need, Praiseworthy. Charity is not measured by what is easiest to part with.
verses.268: Satan threatens you with poverty and commands you toward immorality, while Allah promises you forgiveness from Himself and abundance — Allah is All-Encompassing, Knowing. Two very different futures are offered, one built on fear and one on trust.
